coredata.dc:title: Label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots coupling catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for ultrasensitive recognition of human telomere DNA
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shan
Wang, Lumin
Huang, Chusheng
Su, Wei
Xiao, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive determination of human telomere DNA is quite important in early diagnosis of generalized diseases and biodefense applications. Herein, we present a label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive determination of human telomere DNA based on amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) coupling catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for the first time. During this strategy, af-GQDs are utilized as the reference fluorophore and 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) is chosen as the specific response signal. The fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm increases significantly with a simultaneous fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs at 440nm with the increase of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, resulting in a label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor toward human telomere DNA. This nanosensor exhibits broad linear range (0.2 pM∼50 pM), excellent sensitivity toward human telomere DNA recognition (LOD=25fM), and excellent selectivity for human telomere DNA over some biomolecules. This strategy is promising for the detection of any short oligonucleotides by altering the target recognition unit and avoiding the complex modification process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme
Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
Human telomere DNA
Recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility for human telomere DNA detection
Optimization for human telomere DNA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of af-GQDs, excitation spectrum of af-GQDs at λ em =440nm and emission spectrum of af-GQDs at λ ex =346nm. Insert: Photograph of af-GQDs under white light (Left) and 365nm UV lamp radiation (Right). (B) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs under different excitation wavelength. (C) FT-IR spectrum of af-GQDs. (D) Fluorescence decay trace of af-GQDs. The measurement was made at λ em =440nm. τ is the fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs, and b is the normalized preexponential factor, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs, DAP, and af-GQDs-DAP. (B) Fluorescence decay traces of af-GQDs without and with the addition of DAP with different concentrations. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of af-GQDs, DAP, af-GQDs-DAP system, and summed UV–vis absorption spectrum between af-GQDs and DAP, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs, af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system, and af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system with different concentrations of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. (B) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs under different concentrations of hemin. a∼t: 0, 0.7, 0.9, 1.5, 2, 2.7, 3.5, 4.5, 6, 7.5, 9, 10, 16, 30, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100μM. (C) Relationship between (I 0/I)−1 and hemin concentration in the range of 0.7μM to 100μM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system in different concentrations of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. a∼j: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 1, 1.5, 2.5, 6, 20, 50 pM. (B) Photograph of ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor in the presence of different G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme concentrations under white light (Upper) and 365nm UV lamp radiation (Below). a∼j: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 1, 1.5, 2.5, 6, 20, 50 pM. (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 and human telomere DNA concentration in the range of 0.2 pM∼50 pM.
(A) Preparation and purification of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. (B) Schematic illustration of human telomere DNA determination using af-GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor.
Comparison of methods for the determination of short oligonucleotides.

row.entry: Atomic fluorescence spectrometry with chemical/cold vapor generation
Dextran-hybridization chain reaction using Cy3 as fluorescent probe
Core-shell Fe3O4 polydopamine nanoparticles and hybridization chain reaction
Positively-charged polymer (poly quaternized-(dimethylamino-ethyl)methacrylate, PQDMAEMA) modified screen-printed carbon electrode
Carbon nanotube-DNA nanowire devices and oligonuclegtide-functionalized enayme probe

head.ce:title: Label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots coupling catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for ultrasensitive recognition of human telomere DNA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive determination of human telomere DNA is quite important in early diagnosis of generalized diseases and biodefense applications. Herein, we present a label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive determination of human telomere DNA based on amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) coupling catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for the first time. During this strategy, af-GQDs are utilized as the reference fluorophore and 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) is chosen as the specific response signal. The fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm increases significantly with a simultaneous fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs at 440nm with the increase of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, resulting in a label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor toward human telomere DNA. This nanosensor exhibits broad linear range (0.2 pM∼50 pM), excellent sensitivity toward human telomere DNA recognition (LOD=25fM), and excellent selectivity for human telomere DNA over some biomolecules. This strategy is promising for the detection of any short oligonucleotides by altering the target recognition unit and avoiding the complex modification process.

ce:section.ce:para: As an upstart of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) have attracted tremendous attentions in diverse research fields since their discovery [1–4]. With respect to conventional semiconductor quantum dots containing toxic/expensive heavy-metals and normal carbon-based dots, af-GQDs are superior in terms of excellent photostability, exceptional biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and significantly boosted fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, af-GQDs are expected as the most promising fluorescent sensors for some chemical and biological assays. Based on the unidirectional fluorescence variation of af-GQDs in detection process, af-GQDs have been constructed for metal ions [5] and some important biological substances [6]. However, such unidirectional fluorescent signal variation can be easily disturbed by potentially interfering factors including optical path length, drift of light source or detector, and environmental conditions of samples, which undoubtedly limits the accuracy of quantitative detection and arrests the practical application of af-GQDs to certain extent.

Ratiometric fluorescence approach, which relies on the investigation of the changes in the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two separate wavelengths, effectively avoids the shortcomings of the potentially interfering factors and greatly improves the sensitivity and accuracy of the detection method [7]. Till now, several ratiometric fluorescence sensors are designed and utilized for detection of different targets based on graphene quantum dots [8,9] and carbon dots [10–12]. Especially, our group constructed a novel and label-free ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on af-GQDs for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase for the first time [6]. Such ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor can not only efficiently avoid the potential interferences from background fluorescence but also significantly enhance the detection sensitivity, undoubtedly exploiting the biological applications of af-GQDs.

Human telomere DNA, which is the repeated (TTAGGG)n sequences with associated proteins at the end of a chromosome, plays the vital biological functions in cell proliferation and cell cycle regulation in humans [13–15]. The abundant guanine nucleic acid sequences make human telomere DNA adopt a four-stranded helical G-quadruplex structure via Hoogsteen hydrogen bonding in the presence of monovalent metal ions [16]. This G-quadruplex structure of human telomere DNA can effectively reduce the activity of telomerase and subsequently inhibit the activity of most cancer cells. The gradual loss of telomere DNA repeats in cells may result in the aging and tumor development. Hence, ultrasensitive and highly selective detection of specific human telomere DNA sequences is urgent and increasingly important in clinical diagnosis, diseases therapy, pathology, and genetics. Several labeling strategies have been established for human telomere DNA detection by modification with a fluorescent or radioactive tag and through tedious hybridization or polymerase chain reaction process [17], which makes these methods are complicated, expensive, and time consuming for rapid analysis. Naturally, development of a simple, efficient, low-cost, sensitive, and selective assay for trace human telomere DNA detection is greatly needed.

Recently, one important and increasing popular G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with peroxidase activity has received considerable attentions and been explored to detect different targets from proteins [18], nuclease [19], and RNA [20] to small molecules [21]. Similar to most peroxidases, G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme shows high catalytic activities toward specific H2O2-mediated oxidation substrates, such as 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) [22], luminol [23], and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) [24], resulting in the colorimetric, chemiluminescent, and fluorescent biosensing platforms based on G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. Among them, fluorescent biosensing platforms have recently attracted an increasing interest due to its remarkable properties, such as high sensitivity, wide calibration range, and simple instrumentation. Therefore, the application of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme sensing system with fluorescent detection of human telomere DNA becomes a good choice.

Up to date, although carbon-based nanomaterials are potentially efficient fluorescence probes, af-GQDs never integrate with G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for the design of biosensing platform for human telomere DNA. Inspired by these facts, we fabricate a label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective determination of human telomere DNA based on af-GQDs coupling catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme for the first time. As illustrated in Scheme 1 A, human telomere DNA sequences easily self-assemble with hemin to form G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme under KCl present for catalyzing the oxidation of OPD to its oxidized product 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with the presence of H2O2. The π-network of thick planar sheets in sp2-bonded af-GQDs provides a good substrate for anchoring π-conjugated DAP molecule through the specific π–π stacking interaction [25], leading to the efficient fluorescent quenching of af-GQDs by DAP (Scheme 1B). Therefore, the fluorescence intensity corresponding to DAP at 553nm increases obviously together with a significant fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs at 440nm, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor toward human telomere DNA. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first af-GQD-based label-free and ratiometric fluorescence assay for human telomere DNA. Furthermore, this method possesses other distinct advantages, such as, reliable, ultrasensitive, and highly selective, which may become a facile and powerful tool for any short oligonucleotides detection in bioanalysis and disease diagnostics.

Af-GQDs and hydroxylated single-walled carbon nanotubes (OH-SWCNTs) were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co. Ltd. (http://www.xfnano.com/). The concentration of af-GQDs was 1.0mgmL−1. OPD, DAP, hemin, amino acids, nucleotides, and other proteins were obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Hydrogen peroxide (30%, w/w) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human telomere DNA 5′-TTAGGGTTAGGGTTAGGGTTAGGG-3′ was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technological Co. Ltd. (http://www.sangon.com/) and purified by HPLC. All other reagents were of analytical-reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was produced by passing through a RiOs 8 unit followed by a Millipore-Q Academic purification set (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the whole experiments.

UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded with Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Inc., Australia). Fluorescence spectra were measured on RF-5301 PC luminescence spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decay traces were recorded with Fluorolog-3 system (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). FT-IR spectra were recorded on Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on Chirascan CD spectrometer (Applied Photophysics, England). All pH measurements were made with a basic pH meter PB-10 (Sartorius Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

In order to dissociate any inter-molecular interaction, 100μM of human telomere DNA in 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0, 0.5mM KCl) was firstly heated at 90°C for 15min and then gradually cooled to room temperature for additional 1h to form G-quadruplex. Then, 50μL of 5μM hemin solution was added into 50μL of the above G-quadruplex solution. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 1h to form 100μL G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. Continuously, 200μL of 32.5μgmL−1 OH-SWCNTs was added into 100μL G-quadruplex/hemin solution and incubated for another 30min. As a result, the superfluous hemin was arrested on the surface of OH-SWCNTs and isolated by centrifugation at 12000g for 20min. Finally, the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme was obtained and stored at 4°C until application.

In a typical run for the determination of human telomere DNA, 0.3mL of 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0, 0.5mM KCl), 0.1mL of 40mM OPD solution, 0.14mL of 50mM H2O2 solution, and appropriate aliquot of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme solution were mixed thoroughly in a 1.5mL eppendorf tube and the mixture was stirred at 30°C for 30min in dark. After that, 17μL of 1.0mgmL−1 af-GQDs solution was added into the mixture and finally diluted to 1mL with 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0, 0.5mM KCl). After 1min incubation at 30°C in dark, the fluorescence spectra were measured for quantitative analysis of human telomere DNA.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded from 360nm to 680nm under excitation wavelength of 346nm. The band-slits of excitation and emission were set as 10.0nm and 5.0nm, respectively. The sensitivity was fixed on high and the response time was set as 0.5s. The fluorescence intensities of af-GQDs at 440nm (I 440) and DAP at 553nm (I 553) were recorded. The ratiometric fluorescence signal variations at I 553/I 440 were utilized for label-free, ultrasensitive, and highly selective determination of human telomere DNA.

The optical properties of af-GQDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic characterizations. As exhibited in Fig. 1 A, a typical absorption peak centered at 330nm of af-GQDs is ascribed to the amino edge functional groups and π–π ∗ electronic transition in CC sp2 carbon domains that leads to the strong fluorescence of these carbon-based nanomaterials [1,3]. The digital pictures inserted in Fig. 1A show that almost colorless af-GQDs solution emits strong blue fluorescence under the radiation of 365nm UV lamp, demonstrating the good photoluminescent property of these af-GQDs. The relative quantum yield (QY) of af-GQDs is calculated to be about 18% under 346nm excitation by using quinine sulfate (QY=54% in 0.1M H2SO4 solution) as the standard substance (Fig. S1), which is much higher than some reported GQDs [24]. Furthermore, these af-GQDs show narrow and symmetrical fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. Same with most of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, af-GQDs undoubtedly possess the excitation-dependent fluorescence property due to the different surface state emissive trap [1,3]. With the excitation wavelength increases from 300nm to 390nm, the maximum emission peak position of af-GQDs red shifts obviously, and the emission peak intensity of af-GQDs reaches maximum at the irradiation of 346nm (Fig. 1B). These results elucidate that the optimal excitation/emission wavelengths of af-GQDs are 346nm/440nm, respectively.

The functional groups of af-GQDs were further characterized by FT-IR spectra. As indicated in Fig. 1C, the obvious FT-IR absorption peaks of af-GQDs at 3431cm−1 and 3131cm−1 are all ascribed to the stretching vibration of N−H bond, while the absorption peak at 1576cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of NH bond [3]. The absorption peaks at 1400cm−1 and 1654cm−1 are responsible for the stretching vibrations of CN bond and HNCO bond, respectively [5], indicating the existence of amines and amides. Since the amino groups are preponderantly located in the edges of graphene layers and no significant basal plane functionalization is existed, electron-donating amino groups have excellent ability to donate electrons than hydroxyl groups and can markedly enhance the photoluminescence of af-GQDs. The fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs is further investigated by time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. The fluorescence data are fitted well with biexponential equation, and the average fluorescence lifetime is given by the sum of ∑b i τ i products [26]. As exhibited in Fig. 1D, the average fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs is calculated to be 6.15ns, suggesting the good photoluminescent property of af-GQDs. Furthermore, af-GQDs show good fluorescence stability for long days, wide pH range, and even in 15M NaCl solution (Fig. S2–S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility for human telomere DNA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we developed a label-free and ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for human telomere DNA by using af-GQDs coupling G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with peroxidase-like activity. As illustrated in Scheme 1A, human telomere DNA with repeated (TTAGGG)n sequences can form G-quadruplex structure in the presence of KCl. Thus, the restricted G-quadruplex structure can intercalate with hemin to form the activated G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme through π–π stacking interaction. Superfluous hemin is removed by the adsorption of OH-SWCNTs. G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with peroxidase-like activity can catalyze the oxidation of OPD to DAP under the addition of H2O2. Our previous report shows that OPD hardly affects the fluorescence of af-GQDs but its oxidation product DAP can efficiently quench the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs [6]. The primary π-network at af-GQDs surface plays vital role of the specific recognition of DAP by the characteristic π–π stacking interaction and electrostatic adhesion, which results in the fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs and the fluorescence enhancement of DAP. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm quenches drastically together with the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm increases significantly when DAP is added into af-GQDs solution, leading to a ratiometric fluorescent change between af-GQDs and DAP. The binding interaction between af-GQDs and DAP is continuously elucidated by calculating the fluorescence lifetime variation and recording UV–vis absorption spectrum change. As indicated in Fig. 2B, the average fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs remains unchanged even after the addition of 30μM DAP, suggesting the ground state complex formation and the static quenching mechanism between af-GQDs and DAP. Fig. 2C shows that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of af-GQDs-DAP system is different with the summed UV–vis absorption spectrum between af-GQDs and DAP within the experimental error, further suggesting the ground state complex formation of af-GQDs with DAP. Therefore, we can interpret the fluorescence behavior of the af-GQDs-DAP system as a result of the static fluorescence quenching by the complexation reaction between af-GQDs and DAP.

DAP is the oxidized product of OPD with the presence of H2O2 under the catalyzing of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme [24], so the interferences of these co-existing substances on human telomere DNA detection must be investigated thoroughly. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm changes slightly in the presences of human telomere DNA, G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, OPD, and H2O2, respectively (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm can not change dramatically even when OPD and H2O2 are all present in solution simultaneously (Fig. 3 A). However, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm is quenched gradually with the increase of the concentration of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. 2.5 pM and 20 pM of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme present a remarkable quenching effect of 38% and 63% on af-GQDs, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm increases by about 2.5 or 4.2 times. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 is directly proportion to the concentration of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, indicating that this ratiometric fluorescence approach can be used for the indirect and ultrasensitive detection of human telomere DNA.

Hemin, as the cofactor of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, shows great influence on the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs. As exhibited in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm is quenched drastically at the present of hemin alone, which leads to an additional fluorescence quenching strategy for hemin detection. The response to fluorescence quenching of various concentration of hemin is analyzed using the Stern-Volmere equation [27]:
(1)
I 0
− I
I
=
K SV
[ Q ]
=
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
Herein, I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of af-GQDs at 440nm in the absence and presence of hemin, respectively. K SV represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of hemin, K q is the bimolecular quenching constant, and τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs without hemin. Fig. 3C shows the Stern-Volmer plot of I 0/I versus the concentration of hemin with a good correlation coefficient of 0.9997 in the concentration range of 0.7μM∼10μM. From the slope of the linear plot, the detection limit (3σ/k, σ and k are the standard deviation of 12 blank measurements and the slope of the linear plot, respectively) of hemin is 88nM. Moreover, the relative standard deviation for twelve replicate measurements of 2μM hemin is 0.3%. According to the linear regression of the plot, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant K SV is calculated to be 1.37×105 M−1 and the bimolecular quenching constant K q is calculated to be 2.2×1013 M−1 s−1 (τ 0 =6.15ns). Since the K q value of diffusion controlled quenching process is less than 1.0×1010 M−1 s−1 [27], the higher K q value in af-GQDs-hemin system suggests that the fluorescence quenching process is ascribed to the static π–π stacking interaction between af-GQDs and hemin [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for human telomere DNA detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to enhance the catalytical activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, we optimized the preparation and purification conditions of DNAzyme, including the concentrations of KCl, hemin, and OH-SWCNTs, by using the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 as the standard. The ratio signal increases obviously when the concentration of KCl is in the range of 50μM∼500μM (Fig. S6). However, the ratiometric fluorescent signal decreases prominently as more KCl is present (>500μM). It is reported that appropriate K+ can stabilize the parallel conformer of G-quadruplex, and excess K+ can lead to the mixed type of G-quadruplex in buffers [28,29]. As proved in Fig. S7, human telomere DNA folds into a parallel G-quadruplex in lower K+ concentration whereas it converts into a mixed structure upon addition of excess K+. Higher amount of K+ will disturb the stability of the parallel G-quadruplex, which may affect the catalytical activity of DNAzyme and lower the sensitivity of this method.

The concentrations of hemin and OH-SWCNTs are further investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 reaches its maximum when the concentration of hemin is 0.25μM. Above results indicate that higher concentration of hemin can statically bind with af-GQDs and quench the fluorescence of af-GQDs dramatically, but 0.25μM hemin can hardly affect the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs (Fig. 3B). On the other hand, 0.25μM hemin is far more than 10nM human telomere DNA for constructing the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. Although excess hemin is necessary to be added into human telomere DNA solution to make sure the complete formation of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, superfluous hemin can also catalyze the oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2 and cause high background signal of this approach [24]. Therefore, the efficient removal of excess hemin in solution becomes a vital role for further improving the sensitivity of the assay. Thus, OH-SWCNTs is used to remove the free hemin in the sensing platform, because free hemin can adsorb on the surface of OH-SWCNTs while G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme still disperses in the solution [24]. Fig. S9 and S10 show that the G-quadruplex structure of DNAzyme remains unchanged while the concentration of free hemin in solution decreases significantly after the treatment with OH-SWCNTs, proving that OH-SWCNTs obviously reduce the background signal from free hemin in the detection platform. Meanwhile, the G-quadruplex structure keeps constant after the addition of hemin, confirming that hemin can stabilize the G-quadruplex structure through π–π stacking interaction [19]. Further shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 reaches its maximum when 6.5μgmL−1 OH-SWCNTs is present. Therefore, the optimal conditions for synthesis and purification of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme are 0.5mMK+, 0.25μM hemin, and 6.5μgmL−1 OH-SWCNTs.

In order to get the maximum sensitivity for human telomere DNA detection with this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor, the following parameters were optimized: (a) Concentration of af-GQDs; (b) Concentration of OPD; (c) Concentration of H2O2; (d) Buffer pH value; (e) Incubation temperature; (f) Incubation time. As shown in Fig. S12 to S17, the following experimental conditions are found to give best results: (a) Concentration of af-GQDs of 17μgmL−1; (b) Concentration of OPD of 4mM; (c) Concentration of H2O2 of 7mM; (d) Buffer pH value of 7.0; (e) Incubation temperature of 30°C; (f) Incubation time of 30min.

Fig. 4 A displays the typical fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system as functions of increasing concentration of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme under the above optimized experimental conditions. The fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm quenches continuously with the gradual increase of the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm upon the addition of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with increasing concentrations, indicating that obvious ratiometic fluorescence variation is mainly dependent on the concentration of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. Since the concentration of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme is the same with the concentration of human telomere DNA, this ratiometic fluorescence variation can be used for human telomere DNA detection indirectly. Furthermore, based on the internal standard of the green emission of DAP (λ em =553nm), a slight decrease of the blue emission of af-GQDs results in an obviously distinguishable color evolution from blue to yellow (Fig. 4B). Therefore, this simple and low-cost visual assay can be used for ultrasensitive recognition of human telomere DNA with the naked eyes avoiding any sophisticated instrumentations and specially equipped laboratories.

Fig. 4C shows the plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 against the concentration of human telomere DNA. The fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 exhibits a good linear response to the human telomere DNA concentration in the range of 0.2 pM∼50 pM, which is described by the regression equation of I 553/I 440 =1.19×1011 [DNA] with a correlation coefficient of 0.9998. The relative standard deviation for five replicate measurements of 6 pM human telomere DNA is 0.9%. Based on three times standard deviation of 11 measurements of ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor without G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, the limit of detection for human telomere DNA is calculated to be 25 fM. As can be seen from Table 1 , the present ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for human telomere DNA sensing possesses a broader linear range and a lower detection limit, which is comparable to the most sensitive approach reported for short oligonucleotides determination [30–40]. In addition, this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor can be used for human telomere DNA detection without the interferences of common biomolecules (Table S1), implying that this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor may be applied to human telomere DNA detection in biological samples with high selectivity.

In this work, we developed a label-free and ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective determination of human telomere DNA based on amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) coupling catalytic G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with several advantages. Firstly, af-GQDs, which are new kind of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, can be used as an ideal signal output for human telomere DNA detection due to their low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Secondly, based on the fact that the benign af-GQDs need not any complex modification process to conjugate with DNA, by combing the merit of the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme-based signal amplification, an ultrasensitive label-free fluorescence approach for human telomere DNA is developed. Thirdly, the application of DAP as the oxidation product of OPD under G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme catalysis realizes the successful establishment of ratiometric fluorescence assay by investigating the fluorescence ratios of af-GQDs and DAP, which further improves the anti-interference ability and the reliability of this strategy. Overall, these researches provide influential insight into any short oligonucleotides detection based on af-GQDs, which may have a broad range of desirable applications of these novel carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials in bioanalytical and biomedical areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21403039, 21563006), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2015GXNSFAA139033, 2016GXNSFBA380118), BAGUI Scholar Program of Guangxi Province of China, and Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecular Chemistry, Guangxi Teachers Education University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots: origin of tunable heterogeneous photoluminescence
Colossal magnetoresistance in amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots at room temperature: manifestation of weak anti-localization and doorway to spintronics
Optically tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Highly luminescent flexible amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots@cellulose nanofiber–clay hybrids for white-light emitting diodes
Highly photoluminescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots used for sensing copper ions
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase
FRET-based mito-specific fluorescent probe for ratiometric detection and imaging of endogenous peroxynitrite: dyad of Cy3 and Cy5
Preparation of graphene quantum dots based core-satellite hybrid spheres and their use as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of mercury(II) ions
Graphene-quantum-dots-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for visual detection of copper ion
A ratiometric nanosensor based on fluorescent carbon dots for label-free and highly selective recognition of DNA
Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and L-ascorbic acid
Ratiometric fluorescent detection of biomakers for biological warfare agents with carbon dots chelated europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers
Nanopore detection of 8-oxoguanine in the human telomere repeat sequence
Monitoring human telomere DNA hybridization and G-quadruplex formation using gold nanorods
Chemiluminescence detection of telomere DNA in human cells on a membrane by using fluorescein-5-isothiocyanate-labeled primers
Monovalent cation-induced structure of telomeric DNA: the G-quartet model
Dendrimer-like polymeric DNAs as chemiluminescence probes for amplified detection of telomere DNA on a solid-phase membrane
Proximity binding and metal ion-dependent DNAzyme cyclic amplification-integrated aptasensor for label-free and sensitive electrochemical detection of thrombin
Construction of metal-ion-free G-quadruplex-hemin DNAzyme and its application in S1 nuclease detection

A novel and versatile nanomachine for ultrasensitive and specific detection of microRNAs based on molecular beacon initiated strand displacement amplification coupled with catalytic hairpin assembly with DNAzyme formation
A fluorometric assay platform for caffeic acid detection based on the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme
Label-free colorimetric assay for base excision repair enzyme activity based on nicking enzyme assisted signal amplification

G-quadruplex DNAzyme-based chemiluminescence biosensing strategy for ultrasensitive DNA detection: combination of Exonuclease III-assisted signal amplification and carbon nanotubes-assisted background reducing
Label-free and ratiometric detection of nuclei acids based on graphene quantum dots utilizing cascade amplification by nicking endonuclease and catalytic G-quadruplex DNAzyme
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer quenching at the surface of graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of TNT
Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy for the detection of intracellular pH with quantum dot nanosensors
Amplified colorimetric detection of mercuric ions through autonomous assembly of G-quadruplex DNAzyme nanowires
Colorimetric split G-quadruplex probes for nucleic acid sensing: improving reconstituted DNAzyme’s catalytic efficiency via probe remodeling
Label-free and separation-free atomic fluorescence spectrometry-based bioassay: sensitive determination of single-strand DNA, protein, and double-strand DNA
Designed diblock hairpin probes for the nonenzymatic and label-free detection of nucleic acid
Ultrasensitive visual detection of DNA with tunable dynamic range by using unmodified gold nanoparticles and target catalyzed hairpin assembly amplification
Hybridization chain reaction amplification for highly sensitive fluorescence detection of DNA with dextran coated microarrays
A molecular beacon microarray based on a quantum dot label for detecting single nucleotide polymorphisms
Molecular beacon-based enzyme-free strategy for amplified DNA detection
Enzyme-free fluorescent biosensor for the detection of DNA based on core-shell Fe3O4 polydopamine nanoparticles and hybridization chain reaction amplification
A programmable Y-shaped junction scaffoldmediated modular and cascade amplification strategy for the one-step, isothermal and ultrasensitive detection of target DNA
Dendritic DNA-porphyrin as mimetic enzyme for amplified fluorescent detection of DNA
Immobilization-free electrochemical DNA detection with anthraquinone-labeled pyrrolidinyl peptide nucleic acid probe
DNA-CNT nanowire networks for DNA detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shan Huang received her PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2009. After a two-year postdoctoral (2009–2011) stay at the Institut Armand-Frappier, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique (INRS) and INRS-Énergie, Matériaux et Télécommunications, Université du Québec, Canada, she returned to China as an associate professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. Her current research interests are the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and application in biochemical sensors.
Lumin Wang is pursuing her master degree in Analytical Chemistry at Guangxi Teachers Education University. She works on the development of novel nanomaterials for fluorescent sensors.

Chusheng Huang received his PhD degree in Organic Chemistry from Lanzhou University in 1998. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of natural products and their application in chemical sensors.

Wei Su received his PhD degree in Inorganic Chemistry from Shandong University in 2007. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional ruthenium anticancer drugs and their interactions with DNA.

Qi Xiao received his PhD degree in Physical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2010. After a one-year postdoctoral (2010–2011) stay at University of Massachusetts, USA, he returned to China as a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional graphene nanomaterials and their application in biological sensors.
